title: LAW FEATURES OF THE ORGANIZATION OF COMMERCIAL PROCEEDINGS IN MODERN GERMANY
author: Piebiak, Łukasz
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
The   aim  of the study is to identify the main features of the organization of commercial proceedings in modern Germany in order to use this experience in modernizing the commercial proceedings system in Poland and Ukraine. It has been established that the introduction of a system of commercial courts by the Act on Strengthening Germany as a Jurisdiction for Judicial Proceedings (2025) is a significant step towards strengthening Germany's position as a jurisdiction suitable for resolving complex and international commercial disputes.    
  The introduction and expansion of the commercial court network in Germany will promote the international integration of jurisdiction in commercial disputes and make ordinary courts more attractive for the resolution of disputes arising in the context of international business relations. The law on strengthening Germany's position as a jurisdiction for proceedings is the first step in increasing Germany's international attractiveness as a jurisdiction for commercial proceedings. The central innovations in the organization of commercial proceedings in modern Germany are as follows: the creation of specialized commercial chambers of first instance at the level of higher regional courts (Oberlandesgericht) to hear certain commercial cases; their creation is optional for each federal state (maximum one commercial court per state); the court may be chosen to hear large commercial disputes (with a claim amount of at least €500,000); the limits of subject-matter jurisdiction are determined by each federal state separately for its commercial court; the law contains a list of categories of cases that may potentially fall within the jurisdiction of a commercial court.   
  The special features of the German model of commercial court organization are as follows: the law allows only one instance for the consideration of facts, which shortens the duration of proceedings; a panel of three experienced specialized judges has been introduced which hears the case as a court of first instance in all cases; the only available means of appealing commercial court decisions is to appeal to the German Federal Court  (Bundesgerichtshof)  in all cases; proceedings may be conducted in English (by agreement of the parties); all stages of the proceedings may be conducted in English, including the submission of documents, oral hearings, and court decisions; nine federal states  (Bundeslander)  have established commercial courts in accordance with the new legislation (Baden-Wurttemberg, Bavaria, North Rhine-Westphalia, Hesse, Saxony, Lower Saxony, Hamburg, Berlin, and Bremen) with commercial chambers operating at the level of regional courts  (Landgericht);  their establishment is optional for each federal state; at the appeal level the usual appeal procedure applies.

keywords: commercial proceedings, commercial courts, federal states, regional courts, Higher Regional Courts, Federal Court of Germany, Code of Civil Procedure, judicial procedure, court procedure, appeal.
